## - FUSION 360 GALLERY: A DATASET AND ENVIRON MENT FOR PROGRAMMATIC CAD RECONSTRUCTION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Parametric computer-aided design (CAD) is a standard paradigm used for the design of manufactured objects. CAD designers perform modeling operations, such as _sketch_ and _extrude_ , to form a construction sequence that makes up a final design. Despite the pervasiveness of parametric CAD and growing interest from the research community, a dataset of human designed 3D CAD construction sequences has not been available to-date. In this paper we present the _Fusion 360 Gallery_ reconstruction dataset and environment for learning CAD reconstruction. We provide a dataset of 8,625 designs, comprising sequential _sketch_ and _extrude_ modeling operations, together with a complementary environment called the _Fusion 360 Gym_ , to assist with performing CAD reconstruction. We outline a standard CAD reconstruction task, together with evaluation metrics, and present results from a novel method using neurally guided search to recover a construction sequence from a target geometry.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The manufactured objects that surround us in everyday life are created in computer-aided design (CAD) software using common modeling operations such as _sketch_ and _extrude_ . With just these two modeling operations, a highly expressive range of 3D designs can be created (Figure 1). Parametric CAD files contain construction sequence information that is critical for documenting design intent, maintaining editablity, and downstream simulation and manufacturing. Despite the value of this information, it is often lost due to data translation or error and must be reverse engineered from geometry or even raw 3D scan data. The task of reconstructing CAD operations from geometry has been pursued for over 40 years (Shah et al., 2001) and is available in commercial CAD software using heuristic approaches (Autodesk, 2012; Dassault, 2019). Recent advances in neural networks for 3D shape generation has spurred new interest in CAD reconstruction, due to the potential to generalize better and further automate this challenging problem. However, learning-based approaches

to CAD reconstruction have not yet had access to a human-designed dataset of 3D CAD construction sequences, instead relying on synthetic data for both training and testing purposes, e.g. Li et al. (2020). The absence of real world data has limited work on CAD reconstruction using common sketch and extrude modeling operations. Instead a focus has been on reconstruction from simple geometric primitives (Sharma et al., 2017; Tian et al., 2019; Ellis et al., 2019) that lack the rich parametric sketches commonly used in mechanical CAD (e.g. Figure 2). As there is no existing learning-based approach to reconstruct sketch and extrude sequences, we take a first step towards this goal by introducing data, a supporting software environment, and a novel action representation for reconstructing sketch and extrude designs.

In this paper we present the _Fusion 360 Gallery_ reconstruction dataset and environment for learning CAD reconstruction. The dataset contains 8,625 designs created by users of Autodesk Fusion 360 using a simple subset of CAD modeling operations: _sketch_ and _extrude_ . To the best of our knowledge this dataset is the first to provide human designed 3D CAD construction sequence data for use with machine learning. To support research with the dataset we provide an environment called the _Fusion 360 Gym_ for working with CAD reconstruction. A key motivation of this work is to provide insights into the process of _how_ people design objects. Furthermore, our goal is to provide a universal benchmark for research and evaluation of learning-based CAD reconstruction algorithms, bridging the gap between the computer graphics and machine learning community. To this end we describe a standard CAD reconstruction task and associated evaluation metrics with respect to the ground truth construction sequence. We also introduce a novel action representation for CAD reconstruction of sketch and extrude designs using neurally guided search. This search employs a policy, trained using imitation learning, consisting of a graph neural network encoding of CAD geometry.

This paper makes the following contributions:

- We present the _Fusion 360 Gallery_ reconstruction dataset containing construction sequence information for 8,625 human-designed sketch and extrude CAD models.
- We introduce an environment called the _Fusion 360 Gym_ , standardizing the CAD reconstruction task in a Markov Decision Process formulation.
- We introduce a novel action representation to enable neurally guided CAD reconstruction trained on real world construction sequences for the first time.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**CAD Datasets** Existing 3D CAD datasets have largely focused on providing mesh geometry (Chang et al., 2015; Wu et al., 2015; Zhou & Jacobson, 2016; Mo et al., 2019b; Kim et al., 2020). However, the de facto standard for parametric CAD is the boundary representation (B-Rep) format, containing valuable analytic representations of surfaces and curves suitable for high level control of 3D shapes. B-Reps are collections of trimmed parametric surfaces along with topological information which describes adjacency relationships and the ordering of elements such as faces, loops, edges, and vertices (Weiler, 1986). B-Rep datasets have recently been made available with both human-designed (Koch et al., 2019) and synthetic data (Zhang et al., 2018; Jayaraman et al., 2020; Starly, 2020). Missing from these datasets is construction sequence information. We believe it is critical to understand not only _what_ is designed, but _how_ that design came about.

Parametric CAD files contain valuable information on the construction history of a design. Schulz et al. (2014) provide a standard collection of human designs with full parametric history, albeit a limited set of 67 designs in a proprietary format. SketchGraphs (Seff et al., 2020) constrains the broad area of parametric CAD by focusing on the underlying 2D engineering sketches, including sketch construction sequences. In the absence of 3D human design data, learning-based approaches have instead leveraged synthetic CAD construction sequences (Sharma et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020). The dataset presented in this paper is, to the best of our knowledge, the first to provide humandesigned 3D CAD construction sequence information suitable for use with machine learning.

**CAD Reconstruction** The task of CAD reconstruction involves recovering the sequence of modeling operations used to construct a CAD model from geometry input, such as B-reps, triangle meshes, or point clouds. Despite extensive prior work (Shah et al., 2001), CAD reconstruction remains a challenging problem as it requires deductions on both continuous parameters (e.g., ex-

tracting the dimensions of primitives) and discrete operations (e.g., choosing a proper operation for the next step), leading to a mixed combinatorial search space. To recover the sequence of operations, traditional methods typically run global search methods (e.g., evolutionary algorithms as in Hamza & Saitou (2004), Weiss (2009), Friedrich et al. (2019), and Fayolle & Pasko (2016)) with heuristic rules to prune the search space (Shapiro & Vossler, 1993; Buchele, 2000; Buchele & Roles, 2001; Buchele & Crawford, 2003). Heuristic approaches are also available in a number of commercial software tools, often as a user-guided semi-automatic system (Autodesk, 2012; Dassault, 2019) to aid with file conversion between CAD systems. These traditional algorithms operate by removing faces from the B-rep body and reapplying them as parametric modeling operations. This strategy can recover the later modeling operations, but fail to completely rebuild the construction sequence from the first step. We instead tackle the task of recovering the entire construction sequence from the first extrusion. Another approach is using program synthesis (Du et al., 2018; Nandi et al., 2017; 2018; 2020) to infer CAD programs written in domain specific languages from given shapes. CAD reconstruction is also related to the inverse procedural modeling problem (Talton et al., 2011; Stava et al., 2014; Vanegas et al., 2012), which attempts to reverse-engineer procedures that can faithfully match a given target. Inverse procedural modeling methods typically work with synthetic data, while our paper tackles tasks on real CAD models and modeling operations.

Compared to the rule-based or grammar-based methods above, learning-based approaches can potentially learn the types of rules that are typically hard-coded, automate scenarios that require userinput, and generalize when confronted with unfamiliar geometry. One of the earliest such works is CSGNet (Sharma et al., 2017), which trains a neural network to infer the sequence of Constructive Solid Geometry (CSG) operations based on visual inputs. More recent works along this line of research include Ellis et al. (2019), Tian et al. (2019), and Kania et al. (2020). Typically associated with these methods are a customized, domain specific language (e.g., CSG) that parameterizes the space of geometry, some heuristic rules that limit the search space, and a neural network generative model (Zou et al., 2017; Mo et al., 2019a; Chen et al., 2020; Jones et al., 2020). Lin et al. (2020) reconstruct input shapes with a dual action representation that first positions cuboids and then edits edge-loops for refinement. Although editing edge-loops of cuboids may be a suitable modeling operation in artistic design, it is not as expressive or precise as the sketch and extrude operations used in real mechanical components. Additionally, Lin et al. (2020) chooses to train and evaluate their network on synthetic data due to the lack of a benchmark dataset of CAD construction sequences, a space that our work aims to fill. Our approach is, to the best of our knowledge, the first to apply a learning-based method to reconstruction using common sketch and extrude CAD modeling operations from real human designs.

## 3 FUSION 360 GALLERY RECONSTRUCTION DATASET

The _Fusion 360 Gallery_ reconstruction dataset[1] is produced from designs submitted by users of the CAD software Autodesk Fusion 360. The dataset contains CAD construction sequence information from a subset of sketch and extrude designs. We intentionally limit the data to the _sketch_ and _extrude_ modeling operations to reduce the complexity of the CAD reconstruction task. Figure 1 shows a random sampling of the designs in the dataset. Each design is provided in three different representations: B-Rep, mesh, and construction sequence JSON text format. **Sketch** Unlike free-form sketches, sketches in CAD are composed of 2D geometric primitives (lines, circles, splines etc.), associated dimensions (distance, diameter, angle etc.) and constraints (symmetry, tangent, parallel etc.). Sketch geometry is represented by points, that create curves, that in turn form loops within profiles. The intersection of curves, as the user draws, automatically creates closed loops and profiles that are serialized as both raw curves and trimmed profiles. Sketch profiles form the basis for 3D extrusion as shown in (Figure 2).

**Extrude** An extrude operation takes one or more sketch profiles and extrudes them from 2D into a 3D B-Rep body. A distance parameter defines how far the profile is extruded. A notable feature of extrude operations in Fusion 360 is the ability to perform Boolean operations in the same step. As a user extrudes a sketch profile, they choose to create a _new body_ , _join_ , _cut_ , or _intersect_ with other bodies in the design (Figure 2). Additional extrude options are available such as two-sided extrude, symmetrical extrude, and tapered extrude. The combination of expressive sketches and extrude operations with built in Boolean capability enables a wide variety of designs to be constructed.

## 4 FUSION 360 GYM

Together with the dataset we provide an open source environment, called the _Fusion 360 Gym_ , for standardizing the CAD reconstruction task. The _Fusion 360 Gym_ wraps the underlying Fusion 360 Python API (Autodesk, 2014) and serves as the environment that interacts with an intelligent agent for the task of CAD reconstruction (Figure 3). Specifically, the _Fusion 360 Gym_ formalizes the following Markov Decision Process:
- **state** : Contains the current geometry, and optionally, the target geometry to be reconstructed. We use a B-Rep face-adjacency graph as our state representation.
- **action** : A modeling operation that allows the agent to modify the current geometry. We consider two action representations: sketch extrusion and face extrusion.
- **transition** : _Fusion 360 Gym_ implements the transition function that applies the modeling operation to the current geometry.
- **reward** : The user can define custom reward functions depending on the task. We provide intersection over union (IoU) as one measure to compare the current and target geometry.

## 4.1 STATE REPRESENTATION

In order for an agent to successfully reconstruct the target geometry, it is important that we have a suitable state representation. In _Fusion 360 Gym_ , we use a similar encoding scheme to Jayaraman et al. (2020) and represent the current and target geometry with a B-Rep face-adjacency graph (Ansaldi et al., 1985), illustrated in Figure 3, right. Crucial to this encoding are the _geometric_

Specifically, the vertices of the face-adjacency graph represent B-Rep faces (trimmed parametric surfaces) in the design, with graph vertex features representing the size, orientation, and curvature of the faces. The edges of the face-adjacency graph represents B-Rep edges in the design, that connect the adjacent B-Rep faces to each other.

## 4.2 ACTION REPRESENTATION

In the _Fusion 360 Gym_ we support two action representations encompassing different modeling operations: _sketch extrusion_ and _face extrusion_ .

**Sketch Extrusion** In sketch extrusion, the agent must first select a sketch plane, draw on this plane using a sequence of curve primitives, such as lines and arcs, to form closed loop profiles. The agent then selects a profile to extrude a given distance and direction (Figure 4, top). Using this representation it is possible to construct novel geometries by generating the underlying sketch primitives and extruding them by an arbitrary amount. Although all designs in the _Fusion 360 Gallery_ reconstruction dataset can be constructed using sketch extrusion, in practice this is challenging. Benko et al. (2002) show that to generate sketches suitable for mechanical engineering parts, the curve primitives often need to be constructed alongside a set of constraints which enforce regularities and symmetries in the design. Although the construction of the constraint graphs is feasible using techniques like the one shown by Liao et al. (2019), enforcing the constraints requires a complex interaction between the machine learning algorithm and a suitable geometric constraint solver, greatly increasing the algorithm complexity. We alleviate this problem by introducing a simplified action representation, called _face extrusion_ , that is well suited to learning-based approaches.

**Face Extrusion** In face extrusion, a face from the target design is used as the extrusion profile rather than a sketch profile (Figure 4, bottom). This is possible because the target design is known in advance during reconstruction. An action _a_ in this scheme is a triple _{_ face _start,_ face _end,_ op _}_ where the start and end faces are parallel faces referenced from the target geometry, and the operation type is one of the following: _new body, join, cut, intersect_ . Target constrained reconstruction using face extrusion has the benefit of narrowly scoping the prediction problem with shorter action sequences and simpler actions. Conversely, not all geometries can be reconstructed with this simplified strategy due to insufficient information in the target, e.g., Extrude 3 in Figure 2 cuts across the entire design without leaving a start or end face. Of the design sequences in the reconstruction dataset, 59.2% can be directly converted to a face extrusion sequence. We estimate that approximately 80% of designs in our dataset can be reconstructed by finding alternative construction sequences.

## 4.3 SYNTHETIC DATA GENERATION

The _Fusion 360 Gym_ supports generation of semi-synthetic data by taking existing designs and modifying or recombining them. For instance, we can randomly perturb the sketches and the extrusion distances, and even ‘graft’ sketches from one design onto another. We also support distribution matching of parameters, such as the number of faces, to ensure that synthetic designs match

## 5 CAD RECONSTRUCTION

## 5.1 TASK

The goal of CAD reconstruction is to recover the sequence of modeling operations used to construct a CAD model with only the geometry as input. This task can be specified using different input geometry representations, including B-Rep, mesh, or point cloud, with progressively lower fidelity. Each representation presents a realistic scenario where parametric CAD information is absent and needs to be recovered. Given a target geometry _Gt_ , we wish to find a sequence of CAD modeling operations (actions) _A_ = _{a_ 0 _, a_ 1 _, · · · }_ that generates an output geometry _G_ , such that every point in space is in its interior, if and only if, it is also in the interior of _Gt_ .

**Evaluation Metrics** We prescribe three evaluation metrics, IoU, exact reconstruction, and conciseness. IoU measures the intersection over union of _G_ and _Gt_ : `iou` ( _G, Gt_ ) = _|G ∩ Gt|/|G ∪ Gt|_ . Exact reconstruction measures whether `iou` ( _G, Gt_ ) = 1. As multiple correct sequences of CAD modeling operations exist, a proposed reconstruction sequence _A_ need not match the ground truth sequence _A_[ˆ] _t_ provided an exact reconstruction is found. To measure the quality of exact reconstructions we consider the conciseness of the construction sequence. Let `conciseness` ( _A, A_[ˆ] _t_ ) = _|A|/|A_[ˆ] _t|_ , where a score _≤_ 1 indicates the agent found an exact reconstruction with equal or fewer steps than the ground truth, and a score _>_ 1 indicates more inefficient exact reconstructions.

## 5.2 METHOD

We now present a method for CAD reconstruction using neurally-guided search (Ellis et al., 2019; Kalyan et al., 2018; Tang et al., 2019; Devlin et al., 2017) from _B-Rep input_ using _face extrusion_ modeling operations. Rather than discovering a sequence of construction by exploration, the agent is trained to match known reconstruction sequences present in the training set using imitation learning. We leverage search at inference time to recover the given target geometry.

**Imitation Learning** To perform imitation learning, we leverage the fact that we have the ground ˆ truth sequence of modeling operations (actions) _A_[ˆ] _t_ = _{at,_ 0 _· · ·_ ˆ _at,n−_ 1 _}_ for each design _Gt_ in the dataset. We feed the ground truth action sequence _A_[ˆ] _t_ into the _Fusion 360 Gym_ , starting from the empty geometry _G_ 0, and output a sequence of partial constructionsˆ _Gt,_ 1 _· · · Gt,n_ whereˆ _Gt,n_ = _Gt_ . We then collect the supervised dataset _D_ = _{_ ( _G_ 0 _, Gt_ ) _→ at,_ 0 _,_ ( _Gt,_ 1 _, Gt_ ) _→ at,_ 1 _· · · }_ and train a supervised agent _πθ_ that takes the pair of current-target constructions ( _Gc, Gt_ ) to a modeling operation action _ac_ , which would transform the current geometry closer to the target. **Agent** The agent takes a pair of geometries ( _Gc, Gt_ ) as state, and outputs the corresponding faceextrusion action _a_ = _{_ face _start,_ face _end,_ op _}_ (Figure 5). The two geometries _Gc, Gt_ are given using a face-adjacency graph similar to Jayaraman et al. (2020), where the graph vertexes represent the faces of the geometry, with vertex features calculated from each face: 10 _×_ 10 grid of 3D points, normals, and trimming mask, in addition to the face surface type. The edges define connectivity of adjacent faces. Inputs are encoded using two _separate_ message passing networks (MPN) aggregating messages along the edges of the graph. The encoded vectors representing the _current_ geometry are summed together ( _hc_ in Figure 5), and concatenated with the encoded vertexes of the target geometry ( _h_[0] _t[· · ·][ h]_[5] _t_[).][The][concatenated][vectors][are][used][to][output][the][action][using][a][multi-layer][perceptron] (MLP), with the end face conditioned on the vertex embedding of the predicted start face.

**Search** Given a neural agent _πθ_ ( _a|_ ( _Gc, Gt_ )) capable of furthering a current geometry toward the target geometry, we can amplify its performance at test time using search. Here we report the result of the most basic of search strategies, random rollout, and provide results from additional search strategies in Section A.3 of the appendix. We let the agent interact with the environment by sampling a sequence of actions according to _πθ_ up to a fixed rollout length of max( _[f]_ 2 _[p][,]_[ 2)][, where] _[ f][p]_ is the number of planar faces in _Gt_ . If the agent is successful at reconstructing the target, we stop. Otherwise, we repeat the process until we exhaust a global search budget.

## 5.3 RESULTS

We now present results on CAD reconstruction using the test set of the _Fusion 360 Gallery_ reconstruction dataset. We seek to understand 1) how state-of-the-art baseline networks perform on the CAD reconstruction task, 2) how synthetic data performs compared to human designs. In each experiment we use different agents to reconstruct a target design, while holding the search strategy constant. For a target design _Gt_ , each agent uses the random rollout search algorithm and attempts reconstruction over multiple rollouts. Each time the agent takes an action during search (a search step), we track the best IoU the agent has discovered so far, and whether exact reconstruction is achieved. We cap the total search budget to 100 steps.

**Baseline Comparison** We evaluate five different agents to understand how state-of-the-art baseline networks perform on the CAD reconstruction task. The **rand** agent uniformly samples from the available actions to serve as a naive baseline without any learning. **mlp** is a simple MLP agent that does not take advantage of shape topology. **gcn** , **gin** , and **gat** are MPN agents that use a Graph Convolution Network (Kipf & Welling, 2016), Graph Isomorphism Network (Xu et al., 2018), and Graph Attention Network (Veliˇckovi´c et al., 2017) respectively. We use two MPN layers for all comparisons, with standard layer settings as described in the appendix. We report the evaluation

Step 20 represents the point where it is possible to perform exact reconstructions for all designs in the test set. We also detail the conciseness of the recovered sequence for exact reconstructions. We note that all neurally guided agents outperform the random agent baseline. The topology information available with a MPN is found to improve reconstruction performance. The gat and gcn agents show the best performance but fall well short of exact reconstruction on all designs in the test set, demonstrating that the CAD reconstruction task is non-trivial and an open problem for future research.

**Synthetic Data Performance** We evaluate four gcn agents trained on different data sources to understand how synthetic data performs compared to human design data. **real** is trained on the human design training set. **syn** is trained on synthetic data from procedurally generated sketches of rectangles and circles extruded randomly (Figure 8, left). **semi-syn** is trained on semi-synthetic designs that use existing sketches in the training set with two or more extrude operations to match the distribution of the number of faces in the training set (Figure 8, right). **aug** is trained on the human design training set mixed with additional semi-synthetic data. We hold the training data quantity constant across agents, with the exception of the aug agent that contains a larger quantity from two sources. All agents are evaluated on the human design test set.

Figure 7 shows that training on human design data offers a significant advantage over synthetic and semi-synthetic data. For the aug agent reconstruction performance is aided early on by data augmentation. This is likely due to semi-synthetic designs with 1 or 2 extrusions appearing similar to human designs. Conversely, semi-synthetic designs with multiple randomly applied extrusions appear less and less similar to human design. This difference in distribution between human and synthetic designs becomes more prevalent as search progresses and adversely affects performance.

**Discussion** For practical application of CAD reconstruction it is necessary to have an exact reconstruction where all details of a design are reconstructed in a concise way. It is notable that incorrect reconstructions can score well with the IoU metric, but omit important design details. We therefore suggest IoU should be a secondary metric, with future work focusing on improving exact reconstruction performance with concise construction sequences. Conciseness should always be considered alongside exact reconstruction performance as naive approaches that only reconstruct short sequences can achieve good conciseness scores.

## 6 CONCLUSION AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS

In this paper we presented the _Fusion 360 Gallery_ reconstruction dataset and environment for learning CAD reconstruction from sequential 3D CAD data. We outlined a standard CAD reconstruction task, together with evaluation metrics, and presented results from a neurally guided search approach. We envision a number of future directions that could leverage the reconstruction dataset: new representations for sequential geometry capable of performing CAD reconstruction and generation from B-Rep, mesh, point cloud, or image data; reinforcement learning approaches that mimic and improvise sequential modeling operations; and sketch and constraint synthesis from 3D geometry or images. Finally, beyond the simplified design space of sketch and extrude lies the full breadth of rich sequential CAD modeling operations.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling and well-motivated contribution to the field of geometric deep learning by addressing the critical scarcity of human-authored, 3D parametric CAD datasets. The writing is clear, professional, and effectively establishes the gap between previous research—which relies heavily on synthetic data or limited geometric primitives—and the complexities inherent in real-world "sketch-and-extrude" construction sequences. The introduction does a commendable job of grounding the "Fusion 360 Gallery" and "Fusion 360 Gym" within the broader context of engineering design and machine learning, clearly articulating how these tools facilitate a transition toward high-level semantic reconstruction. While the scope is well-defined, the most significant risk for the authors lies in the technical execution of the "neurally guided search" and the generalizability of their action representation; further sections of the paper will need to rigorously demonstrate that the complexity of real-world human design does not render the reconstruction task intractable for the proposed neural model. Overall, the foundational framing is excellent, positioning the work as a likely high-impact resource for the community.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a critical gap in the literature by introducing the Fusion 360 Gallery dataset and Gym, offering the first large-scale collection of human-designed 3D CAD construction sequences with ground-truth parametric operations, which holds significant potential for advancing learning-based CAD reconstruction. The manuscript is well-organized and clearly motivated, effectively distinguishing its contributions from prior mesh-centric or synthetic datasets by emphasizing the value of editable construction history and bridging the computer graphics and machine learning communities. However, the most significant issues revolve around data provenance and methodological rigor: given the user-generated nature of the source, the authors must provide rigorous evidence of data quality, cleaning pipelines, and annotation accuracy to ensure the benchmark's reliability against the noise often present in crowd-sourced design data. Furthermore, while the novelty of the action representation and neurally guided search is highlighted, the paper needs to convincingly demonstrate how this approach handles the complex hybrid continuous-discrete search space of sketch and extrude operations compared to established heuristic baselines, and explicitly validate the generalizability of the model beyond the specific distribution of the Fusion 360 Gallery users.

comments.title: Minor grammatical, spelling, and terminology corrections
comments.quote: Recent advances in neural networks for 3D shape generation has spurred... maintaining editablity... The edges of the face-adjacency graph represents B-Rep edges... vertexes
comments.explanation: Consolidates multiple proofreading oversights across paragraphs 2 and 32. Includes subject-verb agreement ('advances...has' should be 'have'), a typographical error ('editablity' -> 'editability'), and standard terminology/grammar corrections ('edges...represents' -> 'represent', 'vertexes' -> 'vertices'). These are minor formatting fixes that do not impact technical content but should be corrected for professional publication standards.

comments.explanation: The authors inconsistently render the modifier across the passage: 'human designed', 'human-designed', and 'humandesigned' (likely an OCR/typo artifact). Standard academic style typically hyphenates compound modifiers preceding a noun. Enforcing a single consistent form will improve professionalism and readability.

Uniformly applying or dropping the hat across the sequence definition and dataset mapping will prevent confusion for readers tracking the symbols.

comments.explanation: Merges two related technical ambiguities in paragraph 45. First, summing current embeddings while keeping target embeddings as a variable-length sequence leaves tensor shapes for the subsequent MLP unclear (padding, pooling, or fixed graph size not specified). Second, the conditional prediction of the end face on the start face is described textually but not architecturally (no specification of whether it uses a shared latent, recurrent step, or output-layer conditioning). Clarifying these implementation details is necessary for reproducibility.

The actual data source comparison is in Figure 7. Correcting this citation mismatch is essential to maintain logical flow and prevent reader confusion.

The phrase 'fall well short of exact reconstruction on all designs' is logically contradictory to Table 1 (~62-67% success). Additionally, the statement about Step 20 ambiguously implies exact reconstruction becomes achievable for all designs at that step, whereas it likely intends to state that Step 20 marks the maximum ground-truth sequence length (full geometry specification). Recommend revising to clarify that agents do not achieve exact reconstruction across all designs, and that Step 20 corresponds to ground-truth construction completion.

However, the preceding description and Figure 5 depict a single MLP outputting the action tuple {face_start, face_end, op} jointly. It is not specified whether this conditioning is implemented via a shared latent variable, a recurrent step, or by structuring the output layer to condition the probability distribution of end faces on the categorical output of the start face. This ambiguity leaves the exact architectural implementation unclear.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for ground truth actions

When defining the supervised dataset D, the actions are written without any hat (a_{t,0}, a_{t,1}). comments.title: Misattributed Figure Reference for Data Source Comparison
comments.quote: Figure 6 shows that training on human design data offers a significant advantage over synthetic and semi-synthetic data.

However, they erroneously cite Figure 6, which the caption and preceding text identify as comparing different agent architectures (gat, gcn, gin, mlp, rand). The actual comparison of training data sources is shown in Figure 7. This mismatch breaks the logical flow between the experimental description and the supporting evidence, potentially misleading readers who follow the citation and expect Figure 6 to illustrate data-augmentation benefits rather than model architecture performance.

comments.title: Contradictory Phrasing Regarding Exact Reconstruction Rates
comments.quote: The gat and gcn agents show the best performance but fall well short of exact reconstruction on all designs in the test set, demonstrating that the CAD reconstruction task is non-trivial and an open problem for future research.
comments.explanation: The authors rightly emphasize the difficulty of CAD reconstruction. However, the phrase 'fall well short of exact reconstruction on all designs' is logically contradictory to Table 1, which reports exact reconstruction percentages of ~62-67% at 100 steps for gat and gcn. 'On all designs' strongly implies a near-0% success rate or universal failure, whereas the data shows substantial partial success. The authors likely intended to write 'fail to achieve exact reconstruction across all designs' or 'do not reach exact reconstruction on all designs'. As written, a careful reader cannot reconcile the prose with the reported metrics, creating confusion about whether the agents succeeded on 0% or ~60% of cases.

comments.title: Ambiguous Claim About Step 20 and Exact Reconstruction Possibility
comments.quote: Step 20 represents the point where it is possible to perform exact reconstructions for all designs in the test set.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify step 20 as a meaningful checkpoint in the rollout process. However, the phrasing is ambiguous. It could be misread to mean that at step 20, exact reconstruction becomes achievable for all designs (which contradicts the experimental results showing ~67% success even at 100 steps). In procedural CAD context, this sentence likely intends to state that 20 steps corresponds to the maximum length of the ground-truth construction sequences, meaning the target geometry is fully specified by the ground truth at this step. Clarifying that step 20 marks where the ground-truth construction is complete (and thus exact reconstruction becomes theoretically possible, regardless of agent performance) would resolve the ambiguity and align the prose with standard sequence-generation terminology.

comments.title: Contradiction between conclusion summary and Figure 9 caption regarding search strategy
comments.quote:
We outlined a standard CAD reconstruction task, together with evaluation metrics, and presented results from a neurally guided search approach.

Figure 9: Qualitative construction sequence results comparing the ground truth (gt) to reconstructions using different agents with random search.
comments.explanation: The conclusion explicitly summarizes the paper as presenting results from a 'neurally guided search approach.' However, the caption for Figure 9 (the only results table shown in this passage) states that the reconstructions were generated using 'random search.' This is a direct logical inconsistency. If the evaluation strategy was constant random rollout (as noted in the paper's assumptions), the results do not demonstrate the performance of a neurally guided search, making the conclusion's summary misleading. The authors should either update the conclusion to accurately reflect the random rollout evaluation protocol used for these results, or clarify that 'random search' refers to a specific baseline rather than the primary evaluation strategy.

comments.title: Missing or misaligned row labels in Figure 9 table due to formatting/OCR
comments.explanation: The table is structured to compare ground truth (gt) and multiple agent reconstructions across four designs (A, B, C, D). For design A, the first data row likely corresponds to the ground truth, but the `gt` label is missing or merged with the `aug` row in the OCR extraction. For designs B, C, and D, the first data row contains only numerical values (e.g., IoU 0.95, Steps 2 for B) without the corresponding `gt` or agent identifier. This obscures which row represents the ground truth baseline, making it difficult to directly compare agent conciseness and IoU against the ground truth. Correcting the table structure or row labels is necessary for accurate interpretation.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of quantitative metrics as 'Qualitative' results
comments.quote: Figure 9: Qualitative construction sequence results comparing the ground truth (gt) to reconstructions using different agents with random search.
comments.explanation: The figure presents a structured table of numerical evaluation metrics (IoU and Steps), which are objective, quantitative measures of reconstruction accuracy and efficiency. In academic writing, 'qualitative' typically refers to visual examples, descriptive case studies, or non-numerical analysis. Labeling a metric-driven results table as 'qualitative' is a terminology mismatch that could mislead readers into expecting visual renderings or descriptive analysis rather than numerical performance comparisons. comments.title: Notation ambiguity in IoU formula
comments.quote: iou ( G, Gt ) = _|G ∩ Gt|/|G ∪ Gt|_
comments.explanation: I initially questioned the use of absolute value bars $|...|$ to denote the volume (or area) of the geometries $G$ and $G_t$ in the IoU formula. Standard set theory notation $|S|$ denotes the cardinality (number of elements) of a set, which is inappropriate for measuring the volume or area of a continuous solid in CAD. While the abuse of notation $|X|$ is common in computer vision to implicitly mean 'size' or 'voxel count', applying it here risks mathematical imprecision in a field relying on continuous geometry (B-Rep). A rigorous definition would use $Vol(G)$ or $Area(G)$.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

**Overall Quality and Clarity:**  
This paper addresses a timely and important gap in the CAD reconstruction literature by introducing the first large-scale dataset of human-designed 3D CAD construction sequences, accompanied by a standardized environment and a novel neurally guided search method. The writing is generally clear and well-structured, with a logical progression from motivation to contributions to related work. Figures are effectively used to illustrate the construction sequence concept and the variety of designs in the dataset.

**Most Significant Issues:**

1. **Limited evaluation and missing results:** The paper promises "results from a novel method using neurally guided search" in both the abstract and contributions, yet the provided excerpt contains no experimental results, quantitative evaluation, or ablation studies. An ICLR submission without any results section or preliminary findings is a critical omission; even early-stage results or a description of the evaluation protocol would substantially strengthen the submission.

2. **Lack of dataset analysis and statistics:** While the paper states the dataset contains 8,625 designs, it provides minimal characterization of the data—no distribution of sequence lengths, complexity measures, diversity analysis, or discussion of potential biases in user-contributed designs. Understanding the dataset's coverage, limitations, and variability is essential for establishing its utility as a benchmark.

3. **Methodological details are insufficiently specified:** The proposed neurally guided search using a graph neural network policy trained via imitation learning is described only at a high conceptual level. Critical details about the state representation, action space, network architecture, training procedure, and search strategy are absent, making it impossible to assess the novelty, feasibility, or potential effectiveness of the approach.

4. **Missing validation of the task formulation:** The paper defines a "standard CAD reconstruction task" and evaluation metrics but does not justify why the proposed Markov Decision Process formulation and metrics are appropriate or how they compare to alternative formulations. Without concrete examples of the metric behavior or baseline comparisons, the task definition remains abstract and unvalidated.

comments.title: Truncated method description for search
comments.quote: The passage ends abruptly with 'Give' at the start of a 'Search' subsection.
comments.explanation: The text ends abruptly after 'Search' with the word 'Give' (likely the start of 'Given'). This suggests that the passage provided is incomplete; the search procedure is not described. Without this, it is impossible to evaluate whether the neurally-guided search method is fully specified or whether key details (e.g., beam search width, termination condition, handling of repeated states, or how the agent's predictions are combined with search) are provided elsewhere. This is a reproduction issue of moderate severity.

comments.title: Garbled rollout length formula
comments.quote: We let the agent interact with the environment by sampling a sequence of actions according to πθ up to a fixed rollout length of max( [f] 2 [p][,] [2) , where [ f][p] is the number of planar faces in Gt .
comments.explanation: The expression for the rollout length is corrupted by OCR artifacts, making it uninterpretable. The intended formula is likely max(⌊f_p/2⌋, 2) or max(f_p/2, 2), but neither can be reliably inferred from the garbled text. Since the rollout length is critical to the search procedure and reproducibility, the ambiguity is a real problem.

comments.title: Underspecified concatenation and conditioning in agent architecture
comments.quote: The encoded vectors representing the _current_ geometry are summed together ( _hc_ in Figure 5), and concatenated with the encoded vertexes of the target geometry ( _h_[0] _t[· · ·][ h]_[5] _t_[). The concatenated vectors are used to output the action using a multi-layer perceptron (MLP), with the end face conditioned on the vertex embedding of the predicted start face.
comments.explanation: The description does not specify how the variable number of target face embeddings (vertexes) is handled during concatenation (e.g., all vertices concatenated, only a fixed subset, or some pooling). It also omits the mechanism for conditioning the end face on the start face embedding. These details are essential for reproducing the neural architecture, and no reference to an appendix section that clarifies them is given in this passage.

comments.title: Misleading claim about exact reconstructions at step 20
comments.quote: Step 20 represents the point where it is possible to perform exact reconstructions for all designs in the test set.
comments.explanation: This statement directly contradicts earlier claims that only ≈80% of designs are reconstructable with face extrusion (Section 4.2) and the experimental results in Table 1, where even the best agent (GAT) achieves only 61.91% exact reconstruction at 20 steps. The phrase “all designs” is therefore inaccurate and could mislead readers into overestimating the capability of the method or the coverage of the action representation.

comments.title: Impossible ground truth IoU in subtable D
comments.explanation: In subtable D, the row labeled 'gt' (ground truth) shows an IoU of 0.37 with 3 steps. By definition, the ground truth construction sequence exactly reconstructs the target geometry, so its IoU must be 1.0. The value 0.37 is almost certainly an OCR misalignment or formatting error (e.g., the IoU column for D may actually belong to the 'aug' row and the 'gt' IoU is missing or placed incorrectly). This can mislead a reader into thinking the ground truth sequence does not achieve exact reconstruction, contradicting the paper's own definitions and evaluation metrics.

comments.quote: Give

Without this, it is impossible to evaluate whether the neurally-guided search method is fully specified or whether key details (e.g., beam search width, termination condition, handling of repeated states, or how the agent's predictions are combined with search) are provided elsewhere. The paper summary indicates that imitation learning is used, but the search component is critical for the claim of a 'neurally guided search' approach. This is a reproduction issue of moderate severity.

comments.title: Claim of 'first' learning-based method on real sketch-extrude sequences is technically accurate but needs precise scope
comments.quote: Our approach is, to the best of our knowledge, the first to apply a learning-based method to reconstruction using common sketch and extrude CAD modeling operations from real human designs.
comments.explanation: The authors claim their method is the first learning-based approach for reconstructing sketch-and-extrude sequences from real human designs. This statement appears well-qualified by the preceding review of related work (CSGNet, Ellis et al., Lin et al. all use synthetic data or simpler primitives). The paper's dataset and environment are plausibly the first of their kind. However, the claim's validity depends on the timing (submission to ICLR 2021) and the assumption that no concurrent unpublished work exists. Given the paper is from 2021, this is likely a defensible claim for the time. The main concern is that the method itself (face extrusion + imitation learning) is presented only partially here due to the truncation, so the reader cannot fully assess whether the method actually works on the claimed real data. This is a minor framing concern — the claim is about the approach in general, not the specific results shown.

comments.title: Ambiguity in description of heuristic CAD reconstruction methods
comments.quote: This strategy can recover the later modeling operations, but fail to completely rebuild the construction sequence from the first step.
comments.explanation: The text describes heuristic methods (face removal) as being able to recover 'later' modeling operations but failing to rebuild from the first step. This phrasing is slightly ambiguous: does 'first step' mean the very first operation in the sequence, or does it mean the heuristic cannot reconstruct the entire history starting from scratch? From context, the authors likely mean that such methods can often recover the most recent extrude operations (by removing the last added faces) but struggle to go all the way back to the initial sketch. The comparison to their own approach, which targets the full sequence, is clear. However, a careful reader might wonder whether the heuristic methods can recover any step other than the last one; the sentence could be read as saying they only recover 'later' (i.e., later in the sequence) which would be contradictory if they fail at the first step. This is a minor clarity issue, not a technical error.

comments.title: Subject-verb agreement error in face-adjacency graph description
comments.quote: The edges of the face-adjacency graph represents B-Rep edges in the design, that connect the adjacent B-Rep faces to each other.
comments.explanation: The subject 'edges' is plural but the verb 'represents' is singular. While this is a minor grammatical issue, it could briefly confuse a reader. Context resolves that the intended meaning is that the edges represent B-Rep edges. However, the error remains in the text.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated estimate for alternative face-extrusion sequences
comments.quote: We estimate that approximately 80% of designs in our dataset can be reconstructed by finding alternative construction sequences.
comments.explanation: The authors provide a specific percentage (80%) for the proportion of designs that can be reconstructed using face extrusion via alternative sequences, but no derivation or reference is given. This claim is used to argue for the applicability of the simplified action representation, yet the basis for the estimate is unclear. Without supporting evidence (e.g., a systematic analysis of the dataset), the reader cannot verify this claim. The 59.2% directly convertible figure is concrete, but the 80% extrapolation is not justified here.
